title: STL: Vectors & Pairs
description: The C++ Standard Template Library is a competitive programmer's superpower. This chapter covers the two most-used STL components: `vector` (the dynamic array you'll use in 90% of solutions) and `pair`/`tuple` (for grouping data without writing structs). Master these and your code becomes shorter, faster to write, and harder to break.

sections.content: Raw arrays are fast, but `std::vector` is nearly as fast (the overhead is negligible for CP constraints) while adding **dynamic resizing, built-in size tracking, and iterator support**. The STL isn't just a convenience — it's a set of heavily optimized, battle-tested implementations that let you focus on the algorithm instead of the data structure plumbing.

sections.label: Memory Model
sections.content: `vector` stores elements in a **contiguous block** of heap memory, just like an array. This gives the same cache performance as arrays. When it runs out of capacity, it doubles its internal buffer — amortized O(1) per push_back.

sections.title: vector Construction & Initialization
vector — Add, Remove, Access

sections.label: erase() Invalidates Iterators
sections.content: After calling `v.erase(it)`, the iterator `it` (and all iterators after it) are **invalidated**. If you're erasing inside a loop, use the return value: `it = v.erase(it);` instead of `it++`. Otherwise you get undefined behavior — one of the nastiest bugs in C++.

sections.title: All Ways to Iterate a vector

sections.label: The size()-1 Signed/Unsigned Bug
sections.content: `v.size()` returns `size_t`, an **unsigned** type. `v.size() - 1` when `v` is empty gives `18446744073709551615` (the max unsigned 64-bit value), not `-1`. Always cast: `(int)v.size()` before arithmetic. This is one of the most common beginner bugs in CP.

sections.title: Vector of Vectors — Adjacency List & Grids

sections.content: 🔗 pair — The Most Used STL Utility

`std::pair<T1, T2>` groups two values of potentially different types. It supports **comparison operators out of the box** — pairs are compared lexicographically (first by `.first`, then by `.second`). This makes sorting pairs trivially correct without writing a comparator.

sections.title: pair — All Usage Patterns

sections.content: `std::tuple` extends `pair` to any number of values. In CP, tuples most commonly store three related values — like `(weight, from, to)` for edges in Kruskal's algorithm or `(distance, x, y)` in BFS over a grid.

sections.title: tuple — Construction & Access

sections.label: pair vs tuple vs struct
sections.content: For 2 values: use `pair`. For 3+ values that appear often: define a `struct` for readability. For 3 values in a one-off context (like sorting edges): `tuple` is fine. Avoid deeply nested pairs like `pair<pair<int,int>,int>` — they're hard to read and error-prone.

sections.title: Pattern: Coordinate Compression
Pattern: Sort with Custom Key (pair trick)
Exercise 1: vector Operations Trace
Exercise 2: pair Sorting & Comparison
sections.explanation: Pair sorting is lexicographic: compare first elements first. Groups: {1:2, 1:4} (second asc), then {2:3, 2:7}, then {3:1}. `{1,4} < {1,5}`: same first, 4 < 5 → true (1). `{2,0} < {1,9}`: 2 > 1 → false (0).

sections.title: Exercise 3: The size()-1 Trap
sections.explanation: `(int)v.size() - 1` = 3 - 1 = 2. `(int)empty.size() - 1` = 0 - 1 = -1 (correct, because we cast to signed int first). Without the cast, `empty.size() - 1` = 0u - 1u = 18446744073709551615 (unsigned wrap-around — a serious bug).

sections.title: Exercise 4: Group and Sort — Leaderboard
sections.approach: Store each player as `pair<int, string>` = `{score, name}`. Sort with a custom comparator: descending score, then ascending name. Or store `{-score, name}` and sort ascending — the negation trick avoids writing a comparator.
sections.explanation: The custom comparator sorts descending by score (higher first) and breaks ties alphabetically. The structured binding `auto& [score, name]` (C++17) reads directly into the pair fields, making the input loop clean. Output for the example: Alice 95, Charlie 95, Alice 87, Bob 87.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Remove Duplicates Preserving Order
sections.approach: Use an `unordered_set` as a 'seen' tracker. Iterate the original vector; if an element hasn't been seen, add it to the result and mark it seen. O(n) average time.
sections.explanation: `set::insert()` returns `{iterator, bool}`. The bool (`.second`) is `true` if the element was newly inserted (not a duplicate). This O(n) approach preserves insertion order. Note: `sort` + `unique` is O(n log n) and does NOT preserve order — a common wrong approach.
